content:
Just shipped a new feature that I've been working on for weeks. 🚀

Here's what I learned along the way:

1. Start with the user problem, not the solution
2. Ship fast, iterate faster
3. Feedback from real users > internal assumptions

The best products aren't built in isolation. They're shaped by conversations, failures, and relentless curiosity.

What's a recent project that taught you something unexpected?

Unpopular opinion: The best engineers aren't the ones who write the most code.

They're the ones who:
→ Ask the right questions before writing a single line
→ Delete code as confidently as they write it
→ Communicate complex ideas simply
→ Know when NOT to build something

Technical skills get you in the door. Communication skills move you through it.

Agree or disagree? Drop your thoughts below 👇

I used to think AI would replace developers.

Now I think it will replace developers who refuse to learn AI.

Here's my framework for staying relevant:

✅ Learn prompt engineering (it's a real skill)
✅ Build with AI tools daily — not just read about them
✅ Understand the fundamentals — AI can't replace deep thinking
✅ Focus on problems, not tools

The developers who thrive will be the ones who treat AI as a multiplier, not a competitor.

What AI tools are you using in your workflow?

3 months ago, I was burned out.

Today, I'm more productive than ever. Here's what changed:

1. I stopped saying yes to everything
2. I blocked 2 hours every morning for deep work
3. I replaced doomscrolling with reading
4. I started shipping smaller, more often

Burnout isn't about working too much. It's about working on the wrong things without boundaries.

If you're feeling stuck, start with ONE boundary this week.

What's yours going to be?

LinkedIn tip that nobody talks about:

Stop writing for "engagement." Start writing for ONE person.

When I write a post, I imagine I'm having coffee with a friend who asked me a question. That shift changed everything:

☕ My writing became more honest
☕ Engagement actually went UP
☕ I started attracting the right audience

Content that connects > content that performs.

Who's the one person you're writing for?
